coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detections for three kinds of vitamin B in aqueous solution and on test paper by fluorescent dual-emission carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mei, Xiping
Du, Qian
Li, Junfen
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Although a few of fluorescent probes based on carbon dots (CDs) for vitamin B (VB) determination have been emerged, none of them can realize the detection of different kinds of VB. In this paper, nitrogen, chlorine co-doped dual-emission CDs (N, Cl-CDs) with emissions at 404 nm and 595 nm have been easily synthesized. VB2, VB9 and VB12 can all induce obvious fluorescence turn-off response toward the N, Cl-CDs. Based on that, three types of VBs are quantitatively and sensitively evaluated in aqueous solution with wide concentration ranges of 14.9–135.0 μM, 34.7–89.8 μM and 29.8–79.8 μM, respectively. Importantly, visual semiquantitative detection of VBs on a test strip are also proposed. Moreover, the current N, Cl-CDs have been successfully applied to the detection of VBs in real samples. The N, Cl-CDs are sensitively multifunctional sensors for three kinds of VBs in aqueous solution and the visual semiquantitative detection by test paper assay is simple, portable and inexpensive.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Double-emission
Carbon dots
vitamin B；fluorescence
Detection
Test paper

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, Cl-CDs test strip
Optimization of N, Cl-CDs synthesis conditions
Characterization of the dual-emission N, Cl-CDs
Spectral properties and stability of N, Cl-CDs
Selectivity and anti-interference of sensing VBs
Fluorescence detection of vitamin B in solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090). The authors also appreciate the help of the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and HRTEM image of N, Cl-CDs (inset). (b) The size distribution histogram of N, Cl-CDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum. The high-resolution spectrum of (b) C1s; (c) Cl2p; (d) FT-IR spectrum of N, Cl-CDs.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum (λex = 320 nm) of the N, Cl-CDs. Inset: photographs of the N, Cl-CDs under sunlight (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation (right). (b) The emission spectra of the N, Cl-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Fluorescence intensity in N, Cl-CDs solution with VB12 and potential disruptors. The values of F404/F589 of N, Cl-CDs solution in the presence of (b) VB2 (c) VB9 (d) VB12 and potential disruptors. All data were presented as mean values ± standard deviation (SD), n = 3.

The fluorescence emission spectra of N, Cl-CDs when adding different concentrations of (a) VB2 (b) VB9 (c) VB12. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs and concentration of (d) VB2 (e) VB9 (f) VB12. All data were presented as mean values ± standard deviation (SD), n = 3.

Visualization of VBs on the fluorescent test papers. The photos were taken under 365 nm UV light (The concentrations of VB2 were 0, 15, 50, 75, 100, and 125 μM, VB9 were 0, 25, 80, 120, 160, and 200 μM, and VB12 were 0, 25, 60, 90, 120, and 150 μM, respectively, from left to right.).

head.ce:title: Sensitive detections for three kinds of vitamin B in aqueous solution and on test paper by fluorescent dual-emission carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 The illustration of the synthesis of dual-emission type N, Cl-doped carbon dots by a simple hydrothermal route and the implications.

Although a few of fluorescent probes based on carbon dots (CDs) for vitamin B (VB) determination have been emerged, none of them can realize the detection of different kinds of VB. In this paper, nitrogen, chlorine co-doped dual-emission CDs (N, Cl-CDs) with emissions at 404 nm and 595 nm have been easily synthesized. VB2, VB9 and VB12 can all induce obvious fluorescence turn-off response toward the N, Cl-CDs. Based on that, three types of VBs are quantitatively and sensitively evaluated in aqueous solution with wide concentration ranges of 14.9–135.0 μM, 34.7–89.8 μM and 29.8–79.8 μM, respectively. Importantly, visual semiquantitative detection of VBs on a test strip are also proposed. Moreover, the current N, Cl-CDs have been successfully applied to the detection of VBs in real samples. The N, Cl-CDs are sensitively multifunctional sensors for three kinds of VBs in aqueous solution and the visual semiquantitative detection by test paper assay is simple, portable and inexpensive.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin B (VB) plays an important role in cellular metabolism, the synthesis of red blood cells [1], and the release of energy from food [2]. VB includes vitamin B1 (VB1), riboflavin (VB2), vitamin B6 (VB6), vitamin B12 (VB12), niacin (VB3), pantothenic acid (VB5), folic acid (VB9) and so on. Inadequate VB2 can lead to genitourinary syndrome, eye lesions, skin diseases, and different cancers [3,4]. However, excess VB2 causes oxidative damage to tissues and DNA when human expose to the ultraviolet light [5]. Megaloblastic anemia, fetal intrauterine growth retardation, cardiovascular disease, psychiatric disorders, cancer, leukopenia are believed to be related with low VB9 level [6]. In addition, VB12 deficiency or excess can also lead to severe damage in liver, skin and myeloproliferative disorders [7]. Therefore, it is significant to keep the normal level of these vitamins in our body and stay away from those related physiological diseases.

So far, a number of methods for the determination of VBs have been reported, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), ICP-MS, capillary electrophoresis, cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical method, and fluorescence method. However, most of these techniques have limitations such as the reliance of expensive equipment, complex and time-consuming procedures [8]. Therefore, it is still necessary to find a method that is simple, inexpensive, efficient and sensitive to determine the level of VBs in foods or control the content of VBs in the manufacturing process of medicines.

Carbon dots (CDs) are well-dispersed spherical carbon nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm [9], which have received tremendous attention due to easy fabrication, better photostability and biocompatibility, low toxicity, cost-effective, and excellent tunable photoluminescence [10,11]. After more than a decade of research, the carbon source to obtain CDs now is various including a range of small molecules and natural reagents. The synthetic methods proposed for the preparation of CDs are also diverse and relatively easy, including top-down [12,13] and bottom-up methods [14,15]. Among them, the hydrothermal method of bottom-up is simple, pollution-free, and widely used [16].

In recent years, some CDs doped with heteroatoms have been synthesized to determine the content of VB [17–25]. For example, N, S-CDs with strong orange fluorescence were synthesized by Fan group [17] and were used to detect VB12. Multifunctional N-CDs using simple hydrothermal method to detect VB12 in the range of 0.5–35 mΜ were synthesized by Du group [18]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) synthesized by Du group [19] can be used as dual-response nanosensors for determining VB2 and pH values. N, S, I-CDs were prepared by Mu group [20] through simple hydrothermal reactions for the detection of VB9 in a concentration range of 0.1–175 mM. Dual-emitting carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were synthesized by Gao group [21]. The internal filtering and static quenching effect between VB12 and CPDs can effectively quench the blue fluorescence of CPDs while maintain yellow fluorescence. The established detection method showed a linear detection range of 0.25–100 μM. However, most of these CDs are single fluorescence emission which limit the sensitivity of method by the change of the light source, the environment, and the probe content [19,26]. In addition, the above sensors only response to individual VB, and none of them could simultaneously and accurately determine different VB. To the best of our knowledge, no visual detection of VB on test paper has been reported. The multi-functional CDs were only appeared in the selective differentiation and the quantitative detection of three tetracyclines by Miao et al [27]. For the above reasons, it is hoped to develop a ratio-metric and multi-functional CDs sensor with simple synthesis procedures to improve the sensitivity and the efficiency for different VBs detection.

In this work, intrinsic dual-emission N, Cl-CDs with bright orange fluorescence were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using rhodamine B and ethylenediamine as precursors, which showed good water solubility, uniform dispersion and perfect photoluminescence characteristics. The synthesized N, Cl-CDs were double emissive which were benefit to eliminate the effect of light source, the environment, and the probe content on the sensitivity. It was found that N, Cl-CDs could simultaneously response to three kinds of VBs, VB2, VB9 and VB12, and were satisfactorily applied to the fluorescence sensing detection of VBs in aqueous system. In addition, N, Cl-CDs were firstly applied to the visually semiquantitative analysis for the three types VB on a strip of test paper based on different fluorescence colors of N, Cl-CDs. Satisfactory results were obtained in the testing of selected common VB real samples. In short, compared with other single VB detection probes based on CDs, this sensor presented different and sensitive fluorescence quenching response to the three kinds of VBs. In addition, the N, Cl-CDs herein were innovatively applied to the efficient and rapid determination for the three kinds of VB both in aqueous system and on a strip of test paper, showing more simple, economic and valuable properties.

Ethylenediamine was received from the official website of Enokay Reagents (Tianjin, China). Rhodamine B was purchased from McClane Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). All kinds of small molecules and inorganic salts were obtained from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Dipro Tridiz multi-mineral Gummy were obtained from Xianle healthy science Co.Ltd.(contains VB2 0.13 mg/tablet, Anhui, China). Xi Bei Li brand VB9 tablets (0.4 mg/tablet) were obtained from Lvjianyuan Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Xinxiang, China), and VB12 tablets (0.025 mg/tablet) were obtained from Yunpeng Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Shanxi, China). Double distilled water used in the experiment (≥18.25 MΩ.cm) prepared by US Milli-Q Plus. Distilled water was used as the solvent for all tests and characterization process.
The stock solutions of 0.01 M VBs were prepared freshly by dissolving property amount of VBs in 0.1 % HAc or ultrapure water.

Steady-state fluorescence was scanned on an F-4500 fluorometer and time-resolved luminous decay was recorded with an F-900 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2910 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Infrared spectra were obtained using the Scientific Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Corporation, USA). TEM images were obtained by the JEM-2100 High resolution Transmission Photometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on Axis Ultradld (Edinburgh, UK).

Rhodamine B (0.1123 g) and ethylenediamine (30 μL) were dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water. The chemical structure of rhodamine B contains benzene ring, pyridine ring, carboxyl group, amino group and sulfonic acid group, etc. The big conjugate structure and these functional groups can apply effective conjugation structure and N dopption during the preparation of CDs and make CDs possess special properties, such as hydrophilicity, fluorescence, etc. After 5 min of ultrasonic treatment, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted in an oven at 190 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the N, Cl-CDs solution was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the larger particles and placed in a dialysis tube (molecular weight: 500 Da) for 24 h. Solids were obtained after freeze-drying. Finally, N, Cl-CDs was stored at 4 °C for later experiments.

The QY value of the N, Cl-CDs was determined by using quinine sulfate as the standard solution (resolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 0.54). Specific procedures were showed in the reference [28]. Recorded the fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs and quinine sulfate at 350 nm. The fluorescence yield of N, Cl-CDs was measured as 12.1 %.

Fluorescence determination of vitamin B and the interferences experiments were performed in a 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution at pH = 7.4. The spectra were scanned under the excitation of 320 nm with the slits of excitation and emission set as 5 nm and 10 nm, respectively.

30 μL of N, Cl-CDs (1.5 mg mL−1) and 3 mL Tris-HCl buffer solution were successively pipetted into a cuvette and recorded the fluorescence spectra. Then the fluorescence titration was performed by gradually adding 2 μL VB2 (5 mM) solution each time into the above N, Cl-CDs respectively, and scanned the corresponding fluorescence spectra until the fluorescence intensity was no longer quenched (the effect of dilution can be ignored). The fluorescence determination processes of VB9 and VB12 were the same as VB2.

The selectivity of the method for the targeted materials detection should be evaluated. The fluorescence change of the N, Cl-CDs toward each VB (VB1, VB2, VB3 VB9 and VB12), various common biomass (aminopropionic acid, L-arginine, L-cys, bitter almond acid, VC, GSH), metal ions (1000 μM, including Na+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, K+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), and anionic ions (1000 μM, including HCO3 –, ClO3 -, H2PO4 -, S2O8 2-, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2-) was investigated. The concentrations of these materials were almost 9 times of VBs. The fluorescence signals of VB2 (at 404 nm), VB9 and VB12 (ratiometric fluorescence F589/F404) in absence and in the presence of the above materials were collected by the fluorescence machine.

To investigate the influences of the potential interferences, except the above substances, some small biological molecules (VC, GSH, aminopropionic acid, L-arginine, mandelic acid, five times the concentration of VBs) were also selected. The fluorescence intensities of these interferences in the presence of N, Cl-CDs and VB mixture were measured as the above steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, Cl-CDs test strip
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose filter paper was cut into circles and immersed in N, Cl-CDs aqueous solution. After 5 min, removed the paper from the solution and dried in the air. Then dipped these tapes containing N, Cl-CDs in different concentrations of VB2, VB9 and VB12 standard solutions respectively for 2 min, then the strips were removed from the aqueous solution and dried at room temperature. The fluorescence images of the circular strips were obtained with a camera under UV lamp. The method of taking photos under sunlight is the same as above.

Some tablets of VB2 candy was randomly selected and accurately weighed. Dissolved them in a 50 mL beaker with 10 mL 1 % HAc and stirred. After ultrasound treatment, then transferred and diluted the solution in the flask to 100 mL with 1 % HAc solution. The solutions of VB9, VB12 tablets were obtained by dissolving in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and the other steps were the same as VB2. These solutions were all filtered to remove undissolved substances before use. Diluted the solutions to appropriate concentration for determination if needed. Added an appropriate amount of standard vitamin solution to the diluted sample. Then a spiked recovery experiment was conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of N, Cl-CDs synthesis conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optimal conditions for N, Cl-CDs synthesis, the effects of the ratio of the raw materials, the temperature of hydrothermal reaction and the reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the synthetic N, Cl-CDs were discussed.

As shown in Fig. S1a, when the mole ratio of rhodamine B and ethylenediamine was 1:4, the fluorescence intensity at 404 nm reached the maximum, and that of 595 nm did not change significantly, so the optimum ratio of rhodamine B and ethylenediamine was 1:4. Increasing the synthetic temperature from 170 °C to 200 °C, the fluorescence intensity of 404 nm increased firstly and then decreased, while that of 595 nm did not change significantly (Fig. S1b), so the optimal reaction temperature was selected as 190 °C. The raw materials were reacted at a constant temperature of 190 °C for 3 h, 4 h, 5 h, 6 h, 7 h, respectively, and the change of their fluorescence intensities with the reaction time were shown in Fig. S1c. When the reaction time was 5 h, and the fluorescence intensities at both 404 nm and 595 nm were significantly increased to maximum, so the optimal reaction time was selected as 5 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the dual-emission N, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of N, Cl-CDs were studied by TEM. N, Cl-CDs showed good dispersion (Fig. 1 a), at the same time, the average size of N, Cl-CDs was 1.41 ± 0.38 nm within the scope of 0.5 nm to 2.5 nm (Fig. 1b). This was calculated by analyzing the average size of 100 individual N, Cl-CDs (the Nano Measurer 1.2 software). Furthermore, the lattice spacing of N, Cl-CDs was ca. 0.22 nm as visualized from high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 1a inset), corresponding to the graphitic nature of the N, Cl-CDs.

Then, XPS was used to analyze the composition of N, Cl-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, observational scans of N, Cl-CDs in the XPS study clearly showed the main peaks around 531.66 (O1s), 399.25 (N1s), 284.48 (C1s), and 197.37 eV (Cl2p). As can be seen from Fig. 2b, high-resolution C1s spectra at 284.3 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.3 eV conformed to CC, C-N and CO. Cl2p peaks were divided into two peaks at 198.9 eV (Cl2p1 chloride) and 197.3 eV (Cl2p3 chloride) (Fig. 2c) [29]. Therefore, the XPS results confirmed the existence of N, Cl and oxygen-containing functional groups in N, Cl-CDs.

Multiple functional groups of the surface were identified by FTIR analysis (Fig. 2d). N, Cl-CDs has a wide absorption peak at about 2907 cm−1, corresponding to the tensile vibration of OH [30]. The peaks at 1588 cm−1 and 1337 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN, respectively. The peaks of 1180 and 1074 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O. The band of 826 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of NH [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral properties and stability of N, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To well investigate the capability of the N, Cl-CDs to detect VBs, the spectral properties and stability of N, Cl-CDs were examined. The UV absorption peak of N, Cl-CDs was located at around the wavelength of 262 nm, which was related to the π-π* transition of the carbon core. The 351 nm peak corresponded to the excitation spectrum of N, Cl-CDs, and the longer one around 550 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of the surface CO/CN groups [33,34] (Fig. 3 ).

As shown in Fig. 3a, the color of N, Cl-CDs solution was red under sunlight and bright orange under ultraviolet light. The fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs exhibited two emissions at 404 nm and 595 nm. As reported, the dual emissive bands of N, Cl-CDs are attributed to carbon core and surface state [35]. Considering the QY of two emission peaks, short wavelength emission at 404 nm was attributed to carbon core, and another emission at 595 nm with strong fluorescence emission was attributed to surface state.

With the red shift of λex from 300 to 380 nm, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs around 404 nm gradually weakened and the maximum λem underwent a certain degree of red-shift from 404 nm to 490 nm, showed excitation wavelength dependence. This dependence of the N, Cl-CDs was the result of the different emission sites of each sp2 cluster [19,20]. The fluorescence intensity at 595 nm first increased and then weakened, while the emission wavelength changed slightly, showing excitation independence. This phenomenon was possibly due to the relatively uniform size distribution of the CDs particles [33,34]. Thus, the two emission peaks of N, Cl-CDs exhibited different characteristics when the λex changed. To make sure the dual emission wavelength characteristic of N, Cl-CDs and high sensitivity, 320 nm was used as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments.

To investigate the stability of N, Cl-CDs, the effects of ion intensity, xenon lamp irradiation, pH and storage time on the luminescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs were investigated under excitation at 320 nm. As shown in Fig. S2a, when the NaCl concentration increased from 0 to 3 mol/L, the fluorescence intensity remained substantially unchanged, which indicated the stability of N, Cl-CDs in salt solution. As shown in Fig. S2b, N, Cl-CDs solution showed slight change after long time irradiation under the xenon lamp, indicating excellent light stability. The change of the fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs under different pH was also measured. Fig. S2c showed that when the pH increased from 2 to 11, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs did not change significantly. Taking into account the normal physiological pH, 7.40 was chosen as the optimal pH for the next study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of sensing VBs
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity investigation is considered to be important for the application of a sensor in complex real samples. To assess the selectivity of N, Cl-CDs to VBs, other exogenous metal ions, biologically important molecules, and anionic ions were selected and the luminescence intensity changes of N, Cl-CDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 a, it was obvious that except VB2, VB9, and VB12, the other materials hardly quenched the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs, which showed that the N, Cl-CDs exhibited extraordinary selectivity toward three VBs.

Fig. 4b–D showed the results of the interference of VBs by biologically important molecules or ions in the environment. The addition of them did not obviously change the value of F404 for VB2-N, Cl-CDs and the fluorescence intensity ratios of F404/F589 for VB9-N, Cl-CDs and VB12-N, Cl-CDs systems, indicating that the sensing platforms had good anti-interference property. The experiments showed that the N, Cl-CDs sensor can be a reliable platform to realize the selective monitoring of VBs in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of vitamin B in solution
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the capability of the N, Cl-CDs to detect VBs was tried to be examined in aqueous solution. The luminescence spectra of the N, Cl-CDs at pH = 7.4 with the changing concentrations of VBs were presented in Fig. 5 . It can be observed that when excited at 320 nm, the fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs were varied greatly by the addition of different detection targets.

When VB2 was added, the fluorescence of 404 nm was significantly quenched without wavelength shift, and a new peak appeared at 530 nm (the native fluorescence emission of VB2), and the fluorescence at about 589 nm was accordingly enhanced (Fig. 5a). To avoid the interference of the native fluorescence of VB2, the change of the intensity of N, Cl-CDs at 404 nm was chosen for detection. It was found that the concentration of VB2 was significantly and linearly correlated with F404 in the range of 14.9–135.0 μM, according to the formula LOD = 3σ/K, the calculated detection limit (LOD) was 1.53 μM (Fig. 5d). At the same time, the spectral response of N, Cl-CDs to VB2 solution rendered the color change gradually from original orange to bright yellow under ultraviolet light (see Fig. 5 inset).

With an increasing concentration of VB9 and VB12, the emission intensity of both peaks of N, Cl-CDs were significantly quenched. The fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs added with VB9 exhibited a pronounced red shift at 404 nm (Fig. 5b), and the concentration of VB9 was linearly correlated with the ratio value of F589/F404 in the range of 34.7–89.8 μM, with a detection limit of 1.31 μM (Fig. 5e). Similarly, the concentration of VB12 was linearly correlated with the value of F589/F404 in the 29.8–79.8 μM range, and the detection limit was 1.19 μM (Fig. 5f). Due to the different shift content of the emission wavelength, the orange color of N, Cl-CDs solutions in presence of the two detectors had obvious difference, changed to pale pink by VB9, while became gradually darkened by VB12.

The variations in the fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs showed that the interactions of the three kinds of vitamins B with the prepared N, Cl-CDs were strong and slightly different. The rich carbonyl groups on the surfaces of the N, Cl-CDs promoted the interactions of VBs with N, Cl-CDs. On the one hand, the different structures of these VBs led to the different effects on the spectra of N, Cl-CDs. Based on our speculation, VB2 and VB9 combined with N, Cl-CDs mainly via hydrogen bonds, while for VB12, the complexation of Co + with CO groups of N, Cl-CDs should also be considered, which resulting the longer red shift of fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs. In the other hand, surface defects and the quantum size effect are the major models affecting the fluorescence variations of N, Cl-CDs [36]. Above all, the different structures of VBs and the particle size of N, Cl-CDs are believed to play role in the fluorescence variations of N, Cl-CDs.

The feasibility of the proposed fluorescence method for the detection of VBs in real samples was investigated in candy or tablets samples. The addition recovery rate experiment was carried out. As shown in Table S1, the quantitative recoveries for the detection of VB2 and VB9 and VB12 in these real samples ranged from 98.5 to 102.8 %, 98.6–104.3 % and and 99.50 %-101.50 %, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD) were between 1.38 % and 3.25 % (n = 5). The fluorescent probes are efficient and has high precision and reproducibility, they are promising in monitoring of the amount of VBs in drug and food samples.

Furthermore, the proposed analytical method was contrasted to the previous CDs-based fluorescence method for the determination of VBs, and the results were summarized in Tables S2-4. All sensors listed were doped with heteroatoms or modified to improve the PL property and signal response of CDs to the detectors. Most of them emitted blue or green fluorescence, and the targeted detectors were individual VB. Table S2 showed that the linear range of this work for VB2 detection was wider, and the limit of detection (LOD) here was lower than Ag@CD sensor. For the results of VB9 and VB12, the linear ranges of our work were also widened. More importantly, the determinations of multiple VB compounds were firstly realized in our work, thus, the proposed analytical method exhibited good analytical performance and more efficient and valuable in comparison with some methods.

Enlightened by the spectral response and color evolution of N, Cl-CDs to VB2, VB9, VB12, a strategy for the visual detection of vitamin B under 360 nm ultraviolet lamp was proposed. We observed the colors of the test papers under sunlight (Figure S3) and UV lamp (Fig. 6 ). At first, the filter paper soaked with the N, Cl-CDs alone emitted orange fluorescence. After dipping into the increasing concentrations of each VB solution, the fluorescence color of N, Cl-CDs strips gradually changed. When the solutions of VB2, VB9, VB12 reached to 15 μM, 25 μM and 25 μM respectively, the fluorescence of these N, Cl-CDs strips began to show obvious color change in comparison with the photo of the filter paper soaked with N, Cl-CDs alone, which was visible to the naked eyes. That was to say that the dose-discerning ability were as low as 15 μM, 25 μM and 25 μM for VB2, VB9, and VB12. The method could realize the qualitative detection of these VBs and eliminated the tediousness of instrumental assays.

In addition, If increased the concentrations of VB2, VB9, VB12 to 125 μM, 200 μM and 150 μM gradually, the paper strips produced a colored evolution to yellow, pink and dark, respectively. These phenomena suggest that the sensing probes based on the as-synthesized dual-emission N, Cl-CDs can be potentially developed as the visually and on-site semi-quantitative determination of VB2, VB9 and VB12.

Here, the fluorescent sensor was further performed in real samples to prove its visual detection ability for VBs. When the concentrations of VBs in the samples reached to the detection limit of this assay, the strip papers began to appear obvious color change with naked eyes. That was to say that the VBs could be detected easily from the VB2 candy or tablets of VB9 and VB12. Comparing the fluorescent test papers color of VB sample with the tendency of color evolutions in Fig. 6, the corresponding contents detected from VBs in each grain or tablet could be estimated which were consistent with the content indicated on the labels of products (Figure S4). This suggest that the fluorescent test papers can be performed in the visual detection of VBs in real samples. Thereby, the semiquantitative assay on test paper is simple, instrument-free and no need of sophisticated standard solution preparation.

In order to explain the detection mechanism of N, Cl-CDs for vitamin B, some necessary characterization and demonstration were performed to obtain more information. Considering the good linear relationship between N, Cl-CDs and the concentrations of the detector, the fluorescence quenching process was proved to occur through linear quenching models, dynamic quenching [37] or static quenching [38]. The spectra of Figure S5 showed that both fluorescence peaks of N, Cl-CDs overlapped sufficiently with the absorption peaks of VB2, VB9, and VB12, suggesting that their fluorescence quenching mechanism may be caused by internal filter effects (IFE) and/or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [39].

In order to accurately distinguish between static quenching and dynamic quenching, the change of the fluorescence lifetimes of N, Cl-CDs by the addition of VBs was measured, respectively (Figure S6). It was found that the fluorescence lifetimes of N, Cl-CDs changed very slightly by VB9 and VB12. At the same time,as shown in Figure S6, no new peak appeared in the location of the absorption spectrum of N, Cl-CDs after the addition of VB9 and VB12, which mean that the quenching of N, Cl-CDs caused by them was the result of IFE. N, Cl-CDs interacted with the analytes by forming a nonfluorescence ground complex. On the contrary, the presence of VB2 caused a significant change in the fluorescence lifetime of N, Cl-CDs and no new peaks appeared in the absorption spectrum of N, Cl-CDs, so the detection mechanism of VB2 by N, Cl-CDs was the result of the joint action of FRET and dynamic quenching mechanisms.

In addition, it should be admitted that the application of this sensor is limited in the complex system in which VB2, VB9, and VB12 coexist with high content. In the future work, we hope to improve the quantum yield of the sensor and realize more sensitive visual recognition of these VBs in real samples.

In this work, novel dual-emission N, Cl-CDs were synthesized with excellent fluorescence property, and their fluorescence showed obvious quenching response to VB2, VB9, and VB12, so selective and sensitive methods for these VBs determination were established. In view of the simplicity and good analyzing performance, the N, Cl-CDs were applied to the quantitative and semiquantitative determination of VBs in food samples with satisfactory results. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this is the first report on the detection of three different VBs based on one kind of CDs which show potential applications in water medium and on filter paper. In the future work, we plan to lower the detection limitation of our sensor to and realize more sensitive visual recognition of these VBs in real samples. Through adjusting the structures of the sensor or the detection conditions, the applications of this sensor in more complex system are also hoped to be realized.

Xiping Mei: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qian Du: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Junfen Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Chuan Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090). The authors also appreciate the help of the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124230.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
